content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: I was hungry, ___ I ate a sandwich.
distractor_rationale: because introduces a reason, not a result — the sandwich is what happened as a result of being hungry, not the reason for it
but signals contrast, and eating a sandwich is a natural consequence of being hungry, not a contrast
or signals an alternative choice between two options, which does not fit here

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Tom likes coffee ___ he doesn't like tea.
distractor_rationale: and joins ideas that go in the same direction; liking coffee and not liking tea are contrasting preferences
so introduces a result, but not liking tea is not a result of liking coffee
because introduces a reason, and the dislike of tea is not a reason for liking coffee

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Do you want juice ___ water with your meal?
distractor_rationale: but signals contrast between two clauses, not a choice between two drink options in a question
and would mean both drinks are offered together, not as alternatives to choose between
so introduces a result or consequence, which makes no sense in a question offering a choice

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Maria didn't go to school today ___ she had a bad headache.
distractor_rationale: so introduces a result, but the headache is the reason for missing school, not the result of it
but signals contrast; missing school and having a headache are not contrasting ideas — the headache explains the absence
or signals an alternative choice, which does not make logical sense in this context

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The weather was cold ___ sunny, so we still went for a walk in the park.
distractor_rationale: and joins ideas that agree or add to each other; cold and sunny are contrasting weather conditions
or signals a choice between alternatives, but cold and sunny are being described as simultaneous conditions, not options
because introduces a reason; cold is not a reason for being sunny — they are contrasting qualities of the same weather

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Anna bought some bread ___ milk at the supermarket on her way home.
distractor_rationale: but signals contrast; buying bread and buying milk are not contrasting actions — they are two things purchased together
or signals a choice between alternatives, but Anna bought both items, not one or the other
so introduces a result; milk is not a consequence or result of buying bread

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The restaurant was full, ___ we decided to eat at home instead.
distractor_rationale: because introduces a reason; the restaurant being full is the cause, and eating at home is the result — 'because' would reverse the logical direction here
but signals contrast; eating at home is the direct consequence of the restaurant being full, not a contrast to it
or signals an alternative choice presented to someone, not a decision made as a consequence of a situation

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: You should leave now ___ you will miss the last bus to the city centre.
distractor_rationale: but signals contrast between two clauses; missing the bus is not a contrasting idea — it is the negative consequence of not leaving
and joins two actions that both happen; leaving now and missing the bus cannot both happen at the same time — the sentence warns against missing it
because introduces a reason; missing the bus is the consequence of not leaving, not the reason for leaving

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: James studied hard every evening for two weeks ___ he passed his driving test with a very high score.
distractor_rationale: so implies that passing was a direct logical result caused by studying, but the sentence presents both facts as equally important events that happened in sequence, joined as a narrative — 'and' is the correct additive connector here; 'so' would require a comma after 'weeks' and changes the register
but signals contrast; passing with a high score after studying hard is not a surprising contrast — it is the expected outcome
or signals an alternative choice, which makes no logical sense when both studying and passing are stated as facts that occurred

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Lisa wanted to visit her friend in hospital, ___ she was not allowed in because visiting hours had already finished.
distractor_rationale: so introduces a result; being refused entry is not a result of wanting to visit — it is the obstacle that prevented the visit, making this a contrast
and joins ideas moving in the same direction; wanting to visit and being refused entry are opposite outcomes, not additions
because introduces a reason; not being allowed in is not the reason Lisa wanted to visit her friend — the two clauses describe contrasting intentions and outcomes

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Tom likes tea, ___ he doesn't like coffee.
distractor_rationale: and adds similar or additional information, but liking tea and disliking coffee are contrasting preferences, not matching ones
so introduces a result, and disliking coffee is not a result of liking tea
because introduces a reason, and disliking coffee is not the reason for liking tea

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: We can meet at the café ___ at the library — you choose.
distractor_rationale: but signals contrast between two clauses, not a free choice between two options for the listener to select
and would mean both places are chosen together, but 'you choose' confirms only one option should be picked
so introduces a result, and neither location is a consequence of the other

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Sara missed the bus ___ she arrived at work twenty minutes late.
distractor_rationale: because would make arriving late the cause of missing the bus, which reverses the logical order — missing the bus caused the lateness, not the other way around
but signals contrast, and arriving late after missing the bus is the expected result, not a surprising contrast
and simply adds two events without showing that the second is a direct consequence of the first, losing the cause-and-result meaning required by the context

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Anna didn't go to the party last night ___ she had an early start at work the next morning.
distractor_rationale: so would mean the early start is the result of not going to the party, which is illogical — the early start existed before her decision and caused it
but signals contrast between the two clauses, yet not going to the party and having an early start are not contrasting ideas — one explains the other
or signals an alternative between two choices, but the sentence describes a single decision and its explanation, not two options

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: She likes tea ___ she doesn't like coffee.
distractor_rationale: and connects similar or additional ideas, not contrasting ones — liking tea and disliking coffee are opposites
so introduces a result, but disliking coffee is not a result of liking tea
because introduces a reason, but disliking coffee is not the reason she likes tea

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Tom bought some bread ___ a carton of milk at the supermarket.
distractor_rationale: but signals contrast, and buying bread and milk are not contrasting actions
or signals a choice between alternatives, but Tom bought both items, not one or the other
so introduces a result clause, but 'a carton of milk' is a noun phrase, not a result clause

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Maria didn't go to the party ___ she had a lot of homework to finish.
distractor_rationale: so introduces a result, but having homework is the reason she didn't go, not the result of not going
but signals contrast, and the second clause explains why she didn't go rather than contrasting with it
or signals an alternative, but no alternative choice is being offered in this sentence

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The weather was cold, ___ we decided to stay inside and watch a film.
distractor_rationale: because introduces a reason clause, but the cold weather is the cause, and staying inside is the result — the connector must link cause to result, not the other way around
or signals an alternative choice, but the sentence describes what actually happened, not a choice
and simply adds information and does not express the cause-and-effect relationship that the context requires

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: You can take the bus ___ walk to the station — both options take about twenty minutes.
distractor_rationale: but signals contrast between two opposing ideas, but the sentence presents two equally valid options, not a contrast
so introduces a result, but walking to the station is not a result of taking the bus
because introduces a reason, but walking to the station is not the reason for taking the bus

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Anna studied very hard for the exam, ___ she still didn't pass it.
distractor_rationale: so introduces a result, but failing the exam is the opposite of what you would expect from studying hard — a contrast connector is needed
and simply adds information without expressing the unexpected contrast between studying hard and still failing
because introduces a reason, but not passing is not the reason she studied hard

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The manager was pleased with the report ___ it contained all the information she had asked for.
distractor_rationale: so introduces a result, but the second clause explains why the manager was pleased, not what happened as a result of her being pleased
but signals contrast, and containing all the required information is consistent with — not opposite to — the manager being pleased
or signals an alternative, but no choice or alternative is being presented in this sentence

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The shop was closed when we arrived, ___ we had to go to a different one on the other side of town.
distractor_rationale: because introduces a reason, but going to a different shop is the result of the first shop being closed, not the reason for it
but signals contrast, and going to a different shop is a natural consequence of the first being closed, not an unexpected contrast
or signals an alternative choice, but the sentence describes what actually happened as a direct result, not a choice being offered

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: I was hungry, and I didn't eat anything.
content.error_sentence: I was hungry, and I didn't eat anything.
content.corrected_sentence: I was hungry, but I didn't eat anything.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: It was raining, so we went outside.
content.error_sentence: It was raining, so we went outside.
content.corrected_sentence: It was raining, but we went outside.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: You can take the bus but the train to get to the city centre.
content.error_sentence: You can take the bus but the train to get to the city centre.
content.corrected_sentence: You can take the bus or the train to get to the city centre.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: She studied hard, because she passed the exam with a high score.
content.error_sentence: She studied hard, because she passed the exam with a high score.
content.corrected_sentence: She studied hard, so she passed the exam with a high score.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Tom didn't go to the party so he had a headache.
content.error_sentence: Tom didn't go to the party so he had a headache.
content.corrected_sentence: Tom didn't go to the party because he had a headache.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Maria likes coffee and she never drinks it after six o'clock in the evening.
content.error_sentence: Maria likes coffee and she never drinks it after six o'clock in the evening.
content.corrected_sentence: Maria likes coffee but she never drinks it after six o'clock in the evening.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: The supermarket was closed, so we had to walk to the one on the next street because we could buy some milk.
content.error_sentence: The supermarket was closed, so we had to walk to the one on the next street because we could buy some milk.
content.corrected_sentence: The supermarket was closed, so we had to walk to the one on the next street so that we could buy some milk.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: You should call the doctor but make an appointment online, whichever is easier for you.
content.error_sentence: You should call the doctor but make an appointment online, whichever is easier for you.
content.corrected_sentence: You should call the doctor or make an appointment online, whichever is easier for you.

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: I was hungry, ___ I ate a sandwich.
distractor_rationale: because introduces a reason, not a result — 'I was hungry, because I ate a sandwich' reverses the logic: eating is the result, not the cause
but signals contrast, and there is no contrast here — eating when hungry is the expected outcome
or signals an alternative choice between two options, which makes no sense in this cause-and-result context

distractor_rationale: because introduces a reason, not a result — the clause after the gap is what happened as a result of being hungry, not the cause
but signals contrast, and eating after being hungry is not a contrast
or signals an alternative choice, but no choice is being presented here
